1: ʾăšer-ḥoleh ʾāḇîkā — "your father is ill" — the ḥālāh (Qal participle, to be sick/weak) signals the deathbed context. Joseph takes both sons (Mĕnaššeh wĕʾeprāyim) — the positioning of Manasseh before Ephraim in v1 (and again in v13's setup) reflects birth-order expectation; the narrative's reversal of this order in vv14,19-20 is the chapter's theological point. The chapter's drama is structured around the expected eldest-first priority and Jacob's deliberate subversion of it.
2: wayyithazzēq yiśrāʾēl wayyēšeḇ ʿal-hammiṭṭāh — "Israel rallied his strength (hithazzēq, Hithpael: strengthened himself / mustered strength) and sat up on the bed." The Hithpael of ḥāzaq (to be strong) is the reflexive of self-rallying — the same form used for David's self-strengthening (1 Sam 30:6: "David strengthened himself in the LORD his God"). Jacob's rallying of strength at the approach of death for this final blessing is the ultimate covenant-act: he collects himself for one last formal transmission of the covenant-identity.
3: ʾĒl Šadday nirʾāh ʾēlay bĕlûz — "God Almighty appeared to me at Luz." The divine name ʾĒl Šadday (17:1) is the patriarchal covenant-name; Jacob cites the Bethel/Luz appearance (35:11: "I am God Almighty — be fruitful and increase in number") as the covenant-event being recalled and applied to the Ephraim-Manasseh blessing. The perfect tense nirʾāh (appeared to me — Niphal of rāʾāh) is the standard theophanic appearance verb; by citing the past theophany, Jacob grounds the current blessing in divine commission.
4: qĕhal ʿammîm — "a community/assembly of peoples" — qāhāl (assembly, congregation) combined with ʿammîm (peoples) creates the covenant formula "assembly of peoples." The same qāhāl will be the word for the Mosaic assembly at Sinai (Deut 9:10; 10:4; 18:16) and for the gathered congregation of Israel. mōrāšāh lĕzarʿăkā — "as an everlasting possession (mōrāšāh) to your descendants" — mōrāšāh (possession, inheritance) is the covenant-land word used in Ezek 36:12 and elsewhere for Israel's covenant inheritance; here applied to the Canaan promise in its original Bethel formulation.
5: lî-hēm — "they are mine" — the adoption formula. Jacob adopts Ephraim and Manasseh as his own sons, giving Joseph effectively a double-share of the inheritance (two tribal allotments instead of one). The comparison "just as Reuben and Simeon are mine (kĕrĕʾûḇēn wĕšimʿôn yihyû-lî)" uses the firstborn and second-born as the reference point — Ephraim and Manasseh will be reckoned at the same tribal-leadership level as the eldest sons of Jacob. This is the legal basis for the Josephic tribes (the two half-tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh) replacing the tribe of Joseph in the conquest-era tribal structure.
6: ʾaḥărê tôlĕḏōtām lĕkā yihyû — "they will be yours" — any sons born to Joseph after Ephraim and Manasseh will not receive independent tribal status; they will be "reckoned under the names of their brothers" (ʿal-šēm ʾăḥêhem yiqqārĕʾû bĕnaḥălātām). The yiqqārĕʾû (Niphal: will be called) is the covenant-naming verb — to be "called by the name" of someone is to belong to their household and inheritance. The arrangement limits the tribal multiplication from Joseph to exactly two (Ephraim and Manasseh); further sons of Joseph are absorbed into these two tribal territories.
7: kiḇrat-ʾereṣ — "a little distance/short way" — kiḇrāh is a hapax legomenon (unique occurrence) in the sense of a distance measure; it appears only here and in Gen 35:16, both describing Rachel's burial location. The word may derive from kāḇar (to be many/much) suggesting "some distance" rather than a precise measurement. The parenthetical on Rachel's death (māṯāh ʿālay rāḥēl, "Rachel died upon me" — the unusual prepositional "upon me" expressing Jacob's personal grief) is Jacob's final remembered grief, cited here as explanation for why he buries her in Canaan rather than at Machpelah.
8: mî-ʾēlleh — "who are these?" — the question of the nearly-blind patriarch. Jacob's failing sight (v10: kāḇĕḏû ʿênê yiśrāʾēl mizzōqen, "Israel's eyes were heavy from old age" — kāḇēḏ, to be weighty/heavy, the glory-word applied to dimming vision) means he cannot see clearly who has entered. The question is not ignorance of their identity but an invitation for Joseph to formally introduce the sons for the blessing ceremony — the formal introduction is required for the legal adoption and blessing.
9: bĕnāy hēm ʾăšer-nātan-lî ʾĕlōhîm bāzeh — "they are my sons that God has given me here" — Joseph's response uses the divine-gift formula (nātan lî ʾĕlōhîm, God gave to me) — the same formula used for Benjamin (43:23) and for children generally in covenant-birth narratives (29:32-35; 30:6,18,20). qāḥēm-nāʾ ʾēlay wăʾăḇārkēm — "bring them to me (qāḥ, imperative of lāqaḥ) so I may bless them" — the ʾăḇārkēm (cohortative: may I bless them) expresses Jacob's will and intent rather than prediction.
10: kāḇĕḏû ʿênê yiśrāʾēl mizzōqen — "Israel's eyes were heavy (kāḇēḏ) from old age" — the same kāḇēḏ verb used for Eli's failing sight (1 Sam 4:15) and for the weight/glory of the divine presence. The irony of the man who deceived his own blind father (ch27) now being blind himself at the moment of his great blessing is the narrative's structural echo: what Jacob did to Isaac, Jacob will now experience from within — the patriarch whose deception exploited his father's blindness now blesses while blind, and his crossing of hands is deliberate, not accidental. wayyiššaq lāhem wayyĕḥabbēq lāhem — "he kissed them and embraced them."
11: rĕʾôt pānêkā lōʾ pilaltî — "I never expected (lōʾ pilaltî, I did not conceive/think possible — from pālal, to intervene, to judge, to intercede) to see your face again." The verb pālal (Hitpael elsewhere; here Piel) in this context means "to reckon as possible/to anticipate" — a rare use. The divine gift of seeing Joseph again AND Joseph's sons is stated as a double unexpected grace: wĕhinnēh herʾāh ʾōtî ʾĕlōhîm gam ʾet-zarʿekā — "and behold God has shown me even your offspring" (gam, "even/also" for the added grace).
12: wayyiqqaḏ lĕʾappāyw ʾarṣāh — "he bowed down with his face to the ground" — Joseph's prostration before his father is the reversal of the dream-bowing where the brothers would bow to Joseph (37:9-10). The covenant-patriarch receives the prostration of the covenant-heir as a sign of the blessing's weight: the son who was bowed to by kings bows before his father for the patriarchal blessing. ʾet-ʾeprāyim bîmînô mîśĕmōl yiśrāʾēl — the spatial arrangement sets up the narrative climax of the crossed hands.
13: ʾet-ʾeprāyim bîmînô mîśĕmōl yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-mĕnaššeh biśmōʾlô mîmîn yiśrāʾēl — "Ephraim on his right toward Israel's left hand, and Manasseh on his left toward Israel's right hand." Joseph positions the sons so that Jacob's right hand (the blessing hand — right hand = authority, power, honor throughout the OT: Ps 16:11; 17:7; 18:35; 110:1) will land on the elder Manasseh. The spatial logic is explicit: Joseph carefully arranges the sons so the expected elder-first blessing will follow automatically from their positions.
14: śikel ʾet-yādāyw — "he crossed (śikel) his hands" — the Piel of śākal (to act wisely/with discernment; to cross, interchange). The Piel śikel combines the meanings: he deliberately, discerningly crossed his hands. The same root śākal means "to succeed/act wisely" in the Hiphil (Josh 1:8; Ps 2:10; Prov 1:3; Isa 52:13: "my servant shall act wisely" — the Servant Song's opening). The blind patriarch's deliberate reversal of expected hand-placement over the sons is an act of wisdom-insight, not fumbling accident — confirmed by his response to Joseph's protest (v19: "I know, my son, I know").
15: hāʾĕlōhîm ʾăšer hithallĕkû ʾăḇōtāy lĕpānāyw — "the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked" — the Hithpael of hālak (walk): hithallĕkû is the reflexive-iterative — "they walked back and forth / they walked habitually" in YHWH's presence. The same Hithpael appears in the covenant-command of 17:1 ("walk before me") and in Enoch's walking with God (5:22,24: wayyithallēk ḥănôk ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm). hāʾĕlōhîm hārōʿeh ʾōtî — "the God who has been my shepherd" — the Qal active participle rōʿeh (one who shepherds/pastures) as a divine epithet; this is the first instance of God-as-shepherd in the OT, the paradigm that Ps 23:1 ("The LORD is my shepherd") will expand.
16: hammalʾāk haggōʾēl ʾōtî mikol-rāʿ — "the Angel who has redeemed me from all evil." The participle gōʾēl (one who redeems/acts as kinsman-redeemer) is the first use of the gāʾal root applied to a divine agent. The gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer) was the family member legally obligated to buy back land sold in poverty, redeem a relative from slavery, and avenge the blood of a murdered relative (Lev 25:25-28; Ruth 3:9,12-13; Num 35:19). The divine malʾāk as gōʾēl applies the kinship-redemption concept to YHWH's rescue of Jacob throughout his life — the covenant-God as the ultimate kinsman-redeemer. Job's confession ("my Redeemer lives," Job 19:25: gōʾălî) draws on the same vocabulary.
17: wayyēraʿ bĕʿênāyw — "it was evil in his eyes" — the idiom for displeasure: rāʿ bĕʿênê (evil in the eyes of) is the standard Hebrew expression for something that displeases or is judged bad (also Gen 21:11: "the matter was evil in Abraham's eyes"). wayyitmōk yad-ʾāḇîw lĕhāsîr ʾōtāh mēʿal rōʾš ʾeprāyim — "he took hold of his father's hand to move it from Ephraim's head" — the physical intervention: Joseph tries to correct what he reads as a paternal mistake. The verb tmk (Qal: to grasp/support) is the holding-fast verb; Joseph grips Jacob's hand to redirect it.
18: lōʾ-ḵēn ʾāḇî — "not so, my father" — Joseph's correction uses the direct negative + vocative. kî-zeh habbĕḵôr śîm yĕmînĕkā ʿal-rōʾšô — "for this one is the firstborn (bĕḵôr); put your right hand on his head." Joseph appeals to the firstborn-right as the obvious principle governing the blessing's hand-placement. The bĕḵôr (firstborn) right was the legal foundation of inheritance priority throughout the OT (Deut 21:17; the explicit firstborn-blessing legislation). Joseph invokes an unquestionable legal principle — not knowing that his father is not confused but is intentionally reversing it.
19: yāḏaʿtî ḇĕnî yāḏaʿtî — "I know, my son, I know" — the double yāḏaʿtî is the emphatic confirmation: Jacob is fully conscious of what he is doing; this is not old-age confusion. wĕhûʾ yigdal wĕʾûlām ʾāḥîw haqqāṭōn yigdal mimmennû — "he also will become great, but his younger brother will be greater than he." The comparative gādôl / yigdal mimmennû (greater from/than him) uses the same verb of greatness applied to Ishmael's promise (17:20) and to the Abrahamic covenant (12:2: "I will make you great"). ûzarʿô yihyeh mĕlōʾ-haggôyim — "his descendants will be the fullness of nations" — mĕlōʾ-haggôyim (fullness/complete-number of nations) is unique in this sense in the OT; it anticipates Ephraim's territory becoming the dominant northern-kingdom tribal block.
20: bĕkā yĕḇāreḵ yiśrāʾēl — "by you Israel will bless" — the blessing formula itself becomes the standard blessing-formula for later Israel: "May God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh." The Piel yĕḇāreḵ (will bless) is future — the blessing pronounced now will become the proverbial formula for covenant-blessing in Israel (still used today in Jewish Shabbat blessing of children). wayyāśem ʾet-ʾeprāyim lipnê mĕnaššeh — "he put Ephraim before Manasseh" — the deliberate reversal is formally stated: Ephraim, the younger, is given precedence — the Pentateuch's last instance of the younger-over-elder pattern that runs through the entire patriarchal narrative (Cain/Abel, Ishmael/Isaac, Esau/Jacob, Manasseh/Ephraim).
21: wĕʾānōkî mēt wĕhāyāh ʾĕlōhîm ʿimmākem — "I am about to die, but God will be with you (ʿimmākem)" — the covenant-presence formula of accompaniment (ʾĕlōhîm ʿimmākem) is Jacob's final comfort to Joseph: the covenant-presence that has been "with" Joseph throughout his Egyptian ordeal (39:2,21,23: "YHWH was with Joseph") will continue with the covenant-family after Jacob's death. wĕhēšîb ʾetkem ʾel-ʾereṣ ʾăḇōtêkem — "and take/bring you back (wĕhēšîb, Hiphil of šûḇ: cause to return) to the land of your fathers" — the covenant-promise of Canaan-return as the active expression of divine accompaniment.
22: wăʾănî nātatî lĕkā šĕkem ʾaḥad ʿal-ʾaḥêkā — "I give you one šĕkem more than to your brothers" — the word šĕkem is deliberately ambiguous: it can mean "shoulder/ridge" (a portion of land) or be the proper noun Shechem (the city). Jacob gives Joseph "one Shechem/ridge" more than the other brothers — the double meaning is likely intentional, referring to the Shechem-region land that Jacob acquired (33:19) and which became associated with Joseph's bones burial (Josh 24:32: "Joseph's bones... were buried at Shechem in the plot of ground that Jacob had bought"). ʾăšer lāqaḥtî miyyad hāʾĕmōrî bĕḥarbî ûḇĕqaštî — "which I took from the Amorites with my sword and my bow" — the military-acquisition claim creates narrative tension with ch34 (Simeon and Levi took Shechem by violence, not Jacob), possibly reflecting an independent tradition of Jacob's own military activity in the region.
